So I'm gonna tell you guys a story and it's a serious story. So at one point in my business, I'm actually gonna show you the revenue board real quick. I feel like I'm in utopia of just the ultimate metropolis. I felt like my business was going so right. I've never been more proud of myself and it was in this range right here, right? Cause you can see the month before in December, you know, everybody was spending money on Christmas presents and shit. And you know, the next month, they spent a ton of money with me, but here's what happened, okay? In December, I had this thing, it was called the Yacht Mastermind. It was an event and it was hosted with this partner that I'm no longer associated with. But anyway, we hosted this event, it was called the Yacht Mastermind. It was so incredible. I mean, we had 70 people show up to this private event. Everybody paid $2,500 a head to show up. The first day was at a home in Hollywood Hills and I'm sorry, Beverly Hills. And the second day was on a yacht and we had 12 of my personal friends that are all incredible experts, mentors, people like Dan Fleischman, Brandon Hampton was gonna come, but unfortunately he couldn't show from a family emergency. I had an astronaut, a guy who climbs in volcanoes and shit like that show up and speak to people about how to be financially free to go and climb volcanoes and be an astronaut. I had the original hacker of the iPhone GL break. I had people come from all over the founder that make a wish foundation spoke. I mean, incredible people. The modern Annapolitan Hill, Greg Reed, so many other incredible people came to this event and gave us their incredible wisdom, Paul Webb. But here's the thing, that just financially just broke me. It hurt. I actually lost $25,000 on the event, but the following month, I had one of the biggest month in business I've ever had and it relates specifically to the lesson we're about to learn in the products and services category about your product service limit. Ladies and gentlemen, in this exact month, when I did 80 something thousand dollars, I actually broke my business. Let me tell you how and why. Although I did 80 thousand dollars in revenue is always what we seek in the marketplace. What we don't understand is that we have client limits. So as you can see the following month, my business reduced by 50% and the same exact result happened the following month in March there of my business reducing another 50%. That came from me taking on 22 clients in one month. I had 22 people that I had worked with and I had also fired all of my graphic designers, which at the time I fired four people and a creative director in the same part of the month. So in the same time that I was firing people, I also took on new business. I'll never forget that time when I brought on nine new employees and I had to train nine new people. I'd never flown to Las Vegas so many times in my life, not for pleasure, but for business. I was very stressed during this time, even though I was making 80 thousand dollars, but it was also very euphoric. I'm gonna tell you a big lesson real quick in reality versus euphoria as a business owner. Every time that we go into financial affluence and we'll talk about conditions in one of the following videos, we always slip commonly into euphoria if we are not aware and conscious of the fact that we need to be more focused and remain in reality when we go into affluence. You as a business owner will wanna go on trips, spend more money on your girl, buy yourself nicer clothes, buy nicer food, increase your expenses, get that Rolex you've wanted, go and buy a new car, go and increase your rental that you were gonna get for the fucking ultra luxury convertible series. Don't increase your expenses, don't do anything outside of your current means whenever you go into financial affluence because I made that mistake here. I was flying around the country literally almost twice a week, which I still do today, but I was doing it at a rate which was not for business in some cases. Some of it was for pleasure, where I would go and spend a lot of money at different locations such as Las Vegas, or I would go and spend money in California and LA. But once again, I was there for business, but not the entire time. Although I had a bunch of reserves come from this specific month where I made more than $80,000, the following month where my business reduced by 50% came from short-term gains and long-term failure from not understanding that I had a limit with what I could sell in business. Keep in mind, I 100% absolutely accommodate the fact that although I did fire four people and hired nine new ones, and I'm sure that also broke my business, I know it was because of the amount of clients that I took on at one time in particular. You cannot go out of your way and sell nine people at one time in my particular business, and you're a digital agency owner, so it's also your particular kind of business, unless you have a very well-oiled machine of people inside as a team who can take on everybody and do it in high frequency, and it doesn't slow the rest of your projects down that you already have in the works. Here's the lessons of what happened in that specific month. I had more cycles to complete than I've ever had before. My entire team was so stressed out from the amount of work that they had to do that they actually stopped doing work all together almost from the overwhelm that they were in. Nobody was happy, nobody was feeling joyous, nobody had positive things to say. We were losing clients left and right in the exact same month that we were signing them all up. I had clients that I had throughout this entire period that we lost great relationships with great people who we had to burn the bridge for because we simply couldn't deliver on the services that we had sold them on. It was one of the most horrendous times in running a business that I had ever experienced, and it was something that became very reactionary for me that I couldn't really control. It became something that I had to catch up on and I was very affected by, rather than being able to be a cause for it like I preached to you in one of these previous courses. You wanna be a cause and not an effect. And I was being affected, I was not a cause. I had an issue in this specific month once again when every single thing started crashing down on me. As I was losing relationships, I was losing revenue, and I was gonna lose recurring revenue that would come the following month, but my expenses had increased. I'd taken on five new graphic designers if you didn't hear what I had said, and therefore I had increased my payroll by over $10,000 in that two week period of time that was coming up in under four days. I'll never forget the feeling of having all of my money from my bank account, the window, in under a week from having to expense payroll out of my personal funds and profit that I had accumulated from the business through time. At this exact moment, I had over $39,000 in different expenses throughout my business, majority of which was payroll accumulating about $32,000 or sometimes $35,000 of that $39,000. I'll never forget the moment where after that next month's numbers came in and I saw that my business reduced by 50%, what did it felt like just to be aware that I had lost over half of, literally half of my clients that I had had that previous month. I'll never forget what it was like to have the first time in my entire business history a feeling of which resonated very, very true to the exact moment I realized that I wasn't able to deliver at all in my business. My team was so overwhelmed, they were so unmotivated, I didn't have the right people, I didn't have the right training, I didn't have the time, intention or ability to do anything that I needed to do to solve the overwhelming amount of problems that kept stacking up on top of my head. Problem after problem after problem after problem without any ability to accomplish anything associated with a solution to set issues. Month number three comes in January, I'm sorry, month number three comes in March here and one of the worst things happens to me. It's a Wednesday morning and I step off of an airplane or I'm about to step off of an airplane and I just landed in Miami from LA. I'm dating my girlfriend at the time because this is March, which is at this point it's May, it's about two and a half months ago and I'll never forget it. I just got back from my girlfriend's house the night before from Los Angeles and I fly out to Miami and it's about eight a.m. when I'm gonna step off the flight and on Friday, which was two days away, I had to make over $9,000 in payroll and I have people who work for me who have kids who are expecting paychecks in order to pay for their family expenses or in order to just feed themselves or maintain their relationships or some that go to school and need to pay off student loans and things like this. More of the story is, ladies and gentlemen, when you're an executive and you choose to be a digital agency owner, you also take on the responsibility of paying for the team members in which you choose to take on. You are a leader and it's your absolute responsibility at the end of the day. No excuses, whatever it takes to always make payroll. I hold that belief very firm as it's a conviction of mine that I always pay my people. That being said, that morning when I had another 50% of my business reduced and it was Wednesday and I had to make payroll on Friday and I had less than $2,000 in my bank account left for my business, I'll never forget the feeling that I had as I was walking off of the plane. The moment that I actually stepped off of the actual plane right onto the corridor that leads you into the airport, I had tears start rolling down my face. It wasn't a cry, it was more of a sob that I couldn't hold back. It was something that actually baffled me and made me laugh because for the first time in my entire life, I'd actually had the amount of pressure that I had accumulated on top of myself literally feel as though the world was crumbling down upon me because it was. I had $7,000 that I had to make just to pay payroll and that was all I was worried about. And if I didn't make $7,000 or more in that Wednesday from the 8 a.m. time that I stepped off a flight and barely got two hours of sleep on a red eye back to Miami, by 5 p.m. that day, I wasn't gonna be able to make the payroll that I needed by that Friday. And I was gonna have to tell parents, I have kids, but I wasn't gonna be able to give them a check and I don't know when I was gonna be able to. There's two day processing time on transactions when you use Stripe, which is what I use. And that's why I say that Wednesday was one of the most critical days of my life and in my business. That day is the day that woke me up. I had called my business consultant and I had said, Graham, I don't know what's going on but I just got off the plane and I'm crying right now. And I need your help. I don't know what's going on but I can't hold it out and it just keeps coming out of me. I just keep uncontrollably sobbing and I don't know why. I'm having trouble speaking, I'm having trouble being secure, I'm having trouble just being myself because of the pressure that I feel and my expenses are currently far exceeding the amount of revenue I'm making. If you keep in mind where this little bar is at right here, it's a little under the $30,000 mark. It's at about $25,000. If you heard what I had said just a moment ago, I have over $39,000 in expenses that I have. So that very month, I actually burned in my business about $16,000 or so. So I had $16,000 in revenue that I had to expense out of my personal pockets and I had dwindled down to under $2,000 in that very month, just two months ago. I had to make a decision in that exact moment of what I was gonna do in order to change my business and make sure that that never happened again. The next month, I had done over $80,000 in net income and the following month, which is this month currently, as I've discussed before, I'll do over $110,000 in Megalodon and that's not including the consulting charges and other fees that I'll be able to associate with outside of Megalodon for revenue, accumulating over $200,000 in revenue in this one month alone. Sometimes when we hit rock bottom and we figure out things the hard way, such as a product service limit, we figure out what is the best and how is the best way to run our businesses. During that very month, I realized my product service limit. I'd realized that in January, 22 clients was way too much for my business. Nine graphic designers, two web developers, two social media experts, three salespeople, a project manager, a COO, two videographers and whoever else I'm missing, being a digital marketer and I don't know, somebody else I'm missing out of the 19 person staff, couldn't handle 22 people because there was no system, there was no structure, there was too much going on, there was no time for training, nobody could catch up, everybody was affected, everybody was reactionary, nobody was a cause and there was no time to solve the problems and there were too many problems to solve. It was one of the worst experiences that I've ever had in my entire life, not just this business, but my entire life. It actually felt like the whole world was crashing down on my shoulders to the point where I was so unemotionally controlling myself that I had started crying after getting off of a plane from how serious the stress was that I had accumulated and compounded on myself. Some serious stuff, if you don't take this seriously, when you're running your business, I'm talking to you from the purest form of myself right now, from pure experience right now, trying to imploring you. Figure out your product service limit, don't go out of your way to sell 10 people at once in your business thinking that you can do it when you might not be able to, because you don't wanna get stuck with a bunch of people's money and then having to expense all of that money just to service the clients that you weren't prepared to service, to then not be able to deliver for those clients because you didn't have the right system, the right training, the right people, or the right solutions in place, to then have those clients come back at you and have refunds. I just got done refunding the people from that very month of bad business and from the months that followed from still not being able to deliver, just literally about three weeks ago. Take that in. You have to understand that these are long-term effects that you are affected by if you aren't in a position where you are sound, stable, and running an efficient digital agency at all times, and this is one of the most compounding ways that you can destroy yourself through time if you're not aware of it. Product service limits are critical to avoiding failure and avoiding pitfalls, but they feel good for short-term gains. Obviously taking out a bunch of clients and getting a bunch of revenue feels great, and it is sometimes a great decision depending on what you're selling and depending on if it's transactional or recurring. In my case, all these clients were recurring. The revenue was insane. It was awesome. I loved it. The following month and the following month, I fucking hated it, and I'd wish that I never made that decision, but to date, it's made me one of the biggest and best entrepreneurs that I could possibly be or imagine that I could have been just from going through that absolute hardship to never experience that again. I encourage you once again from pure experience. Don't go through the horrid experience of figuring out your product experience, of figuring out your product in a period of time that you can control. Horrid experience of figuring out your product service and deliver for them.
